meta.topic: Negative questions; echo questions; questions with that-clauses

step_1_context.text: We usually make a negative yes/no or wh-question with an auxiliary verb (have, did, would, etc.) + -n’t to suggest, persuade, criticise, etc.:

step_2_explanation.simple_rule: We usually make a negative yes/no or wh-question with an auxiliary verb (have, did, would, etc.
step_2_explanation.formula:
We sometimes use negative words instead of -n’t such as never, no, nobody, nothing, nowhere:
 • Why do you never help?
 • Have you nowhere to go? (or Do you have nowhere to go?)
 Alternatively, but less emphatically or more informally:
 • Why don’t you ever help?
 • Haven’t you got anywhere to go? (
step_2_explanation.common_mistake: We can make a suggestion with Why not + bare infinitive or Why don’t / doesn’t + subject + bare infinitive (but not Why do not / does not.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 27.1.1 A: Can you lend me €10? B: Again? ___ got any money left? (... money left?)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: All are possible negative question forms.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 27.1.2 A: I'm annoyed that you didn't come to the meeting. B: Why? ___ my email on holiday? (... my email / on holiday?)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Why didn't you check? (informal) or Why did you not check? (formal/emphatic).

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 27.1.4 A: I'll just finish my homework before I go to school. B: But ___ to finish it last night? (... be supposed to / last night?)
27.2.1 A: ___ considered you might be wrong? / Have you ___ considered you might be wrong?
27.2.3 A: I spent the night in the railway station. B: ___ anywhere else to sleep? / Could you find ___ else to sleep?
